journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of magnetic resonance imaging : JMRI

title-group.article-title: Detection of Early Knee Osteoarthritis Using Multi-Component T1ρ Mapping

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Radiology, New York University Grossman School of Medicine
2Department of Medicine, New York University Grossman School of Medicine

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Hector Lise de Moura, PhD, Department of Radiology, New York University School of Medicine, 660 1st Avenue, 4th floor, Ste. 440, New York, NY, 10016, USA, hector.lisedemoura@nyulangone.org

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: BACKGROUND:
sec.p: Early detection of knee osteoarthritis (OA) is important. Spin-lattice relaxation in the rotating frame (T1ρ) mapping is sensitive to early cartilage changes, but the mono-exponential (ME) model may be limited. Multi-component models can capture more tissue complexity, but their diagnostic advantage has not been validated.

sec.title: PURPOSE:
sec.p: To evaluate if stretched- (SE) and bi-exponential (BE) T1ρ models can improve early knee OA detection over the ME model.

sec.title: STUDY TYPE:
sec.p: Case-control study.

sec.title: POPULATION:
sec.p: 26 healthy subjects (mean age 51.5) and 26 early knee OA patients (mean age 61.8).

sec.title: FIELDSTRENGTH/SEQUENCE:
sec.p: T1ρ-prepared Turbo FLASH sequence at 3T field strength.

sec.title: ASSESSMENT:
sec.p: T1ρ parameters from three exponential models were adjusted for age. To maximize group separability, the parameters were combined into single discriminators for both global knee cartilage and six anatomical sub-regions. Diagnostic performance was assessed based on the ability of these combined models to distinguish early OA.

sec.title: STATISTICAL TESTS:
sec.p: Parameters were adjusted for age. Mann-Whitney U-test (group comparisons), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve (AUC) with bootstrapped 95% Confidence Intervals (CI). Significance level set at p<0.05, using the False Discovery Rate (FDR) to correct for multiple comparisons.

sec.title: RESULTS:
sec.p: In the global analysis, no model demonstrated significant diagnostic performance (p-values of 0.63, 0.96, 0.63 for ME, SE, and BE). Multi-regional SE model (AUC=0.83,CI [0.72, 0.93]) significantly distinguished OA and healthy groups. Calibration analysis showed the SE model had the lowest Brier Score (0.17), significantly better than the ME model (0.26).

sec.title: DATA CONCLUSION:
sec.p: Sub-regional analysis of T1ρ parameter maps suggests an improvement in diagnostic performance for early knee OA compared to globally averaged measurements. The stretched-exponential model showed the most promise. However, small sample size and wide confidence intervals highlight the need for further validation with a larger cohort before clinical utility claims can be made.

abstract.title: Plain Language Summary
abstract.p: Knee osteoarthritis is a disease that leads to the degradation of the knee joint, and its early detection is important for managing the disease. However, standard imaging methods often fail to reveal subtle changes in cartilage health. The purpose of the study was to see if advanced MRI analysis could improve early disease identification. Researchers compared the standard measurement methods with complex models that map the quality of cartilage in specific areas of the knee. The study results showed that a region-specific model was capable of better distinguishing the early-stage disease signs.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Osteoarthritis (OA) is one of the most prevalent causes of chronic disability(1, 2) and presents a substantial socioeconomic cost for patients worldwide (3, 4). In its early stages, the disease process involves biochemical and compositional changes in the cartilage extracellular matrix, such as proteoglycan depletion and alterations in collagen architecture(5). These often precede the structural damage detectable by conventional radiography or standard magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Although OA currently lacks a cure, identifying the disease in these early, pre-radiographic stages is important to help guide decisions, such as lifestyle changes to slow disease progression and help preserve long-term joint function(6). This necessitates diagnostic tools sensitive to the initial molecular changes within cartilage. Additionally, by identifying early OA, these tools can enhance cohort selection for potential disease-modifying drug studies. Large-scale initiatives, such as the Foundation for the National Institutes of Health (FNIH) OA Biomarkers Consortium (7), have highlighted the critical need for prognostic biomarkers capable of enriching clinical trials by identifying progressors earlier than radiography.

Quantitative MRI methods, such as the spin-spin relaxation time (T2) and the spin-lattice relaxation time in the rotating frame (T1ρ) mapping, offer non-invasive methods to probe the biochemical composition of articular cartilage(6, 8). T2 relaxation is primarily affected by water content and organization/orientation of the collagen fibril network, while T1ρ relaxation is also sensitive to proteoglycans(8, 9). Both T2 and T1ρ, expressed as the relaxation constant in a mono-exponential (ME) model, showed the capacity to differentiate healthy controls from early OA patients, Kellgren-Lawrence scale 1 and 2 (KL1–2), and are also correlated with OA progression(8). Still, their translation to clinical routine has been hindered by a lack of standardized cut-offs and clear evidence of added value over standard imaging(10)

While multi-component models have theoretical advantages, their clinical utility depends on whether the increased acquisition and processing can yield diagnostic improvements. Previous technical studies have established the feasibility and repeatability of acquiring bi-exponential (BE) and stretched-exponential (SE) T1ρ in vivo (11–13) and optimized acquisition parameters to reduce scan times(14). However, these studies were primarily methodological, and to date, there is a lack of studies directly comparing the diagnostic performance of these advanced models against the standard ME model.

In this study, the diagnostic performances of the stretched- (SE) and bi-exponential (BE) models using a combination of each model’s parameters across different cartilage compartments were evaluated and compared to the performance of the mono-exponential (ME) model. It was hypothesized that combining parameters from multi-component models would improve group separation compared to the ME model separation. In addition to the recombination of parameters of each model in the global cartilage, it was also hypothesized that combining the parameters of different regions of cartilage, as separate variables, would further improve the sensitivity and specificity of early OA detection for all three models.

sec.title: Materials and Methods
sec.p: The study was approved by our Institutional Review Board (IRB) and complies with Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) regulations. All subjects provided written informed consent prior to the MRI scans.

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: A group of 26 healthy volunteers (HS) (10 females, mean age 51.5±8.4 years old, mean Body Mass Index (BMI) 27.3, no pain and KL0 on X-Rays) and a group of 26 early knee OA patients (19 females, mean age 61.8±7.6 years old, mean BMI of 27.8, KLs 1–2) were recruited for this case-control study, the demographics are summarized in Table 1. All OA patients underwent standardized, weight-bearing, fixed-flexion posteroanterior knee radiography using the SynaFlexer X-ray Positioning Frame (Synarc), as previously described (15). Inclusion criteria for healthy volunteers were age in the range of 40–75 years, either sex, no knee pain or clinical symptoms of OA, i.e., Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grade of 0, and no abnormal findings on clinical MR protocol, such as meniscal or ligamental tears, assessed by musculoskeletal radiologists in the Division of Radiology at NYU Langone Health. For early OA patients, the inclusion criteria were an age range of 40–75 years, either sex, frequent knee pain on most days of the month for the past year, a KL grade of 1 or 2, and a KL grade of ≤1 in the contralateral knee. The exclusion criteria for both groups were the presence of any joint disease other than knee OA, corticosteroid injections within the previous 3 months, and history of avascular necrosis, Paget’s disease of bone, Wilson’s disease, gout, total knee replacement or plan for replacement within next 24 months, pregnancy, major co-morbidities such as diabetes mellitus, cancer, congestive heart failure and chronic infectious diseases, prior knee surgery, trauma, contraindications to 3T whole-body MRI scanners, and alignment interventions such as insoles and knee braces. 711

sec.title: In vivo Imaging
sec.p: All subjects were scanned at a 3T scanner (MAGNETOM Prisma, Siemens Healthineers AG, Forchheim, Germany) with a vendor-provided 1-Tx/15-Rx knee coil (QED, OH).

T1ρ images were acquired using a 3D Turbo FLASH (11, 16) sequence with matrix size 256×128×64, interpolated to 256×256×64, and with a voxel size of 0.8mm×0.8mm×2mm. Images were acquired without averaging or repetition. The T1ρ preparation used hard 90° pulses for tip-down and tip-up, along with two hard 180° pulses for refocusing. The preparation used a spin-locking frequency (FSL) of 500 Hz.

The spin-lock time (TSL) schedule was optimized using a Cramer-Rao Lower Bound method (CRLB) (14, 16) that given the range for the model parameters, the set of possible TSLs, and the number of desired TSLs, it finds a combination of TSLs that minimizes a loss function. Using an FSL of 500 Hz, it was found that a maximum TSL of 55 ms provided a good trade-off between Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) and fitting accuracy, therefore, a range of TSLs between 0.1 and 55 ms with 0.1 ms increments was considered. This optimization resulted in 7 weighted images with TSLs of 0, 4.3, 9, 33, 33, 55, and 55ms. The use of repeated TSLs was a result of the CRLB optimization and was deliberate, this repetition ensures a better fit by boosting the SNR of the longer TSLs, as shown in a previous study(14). The optimization targeted the BE model, which requires the highest number of TSLs. The scan time for each image was 3 minutes and 12 seconds, totaling 22 minutes and 24 seconds for the 7 TSLs.

sec.title: Signal modeling and Image processing
sec.p: T1ρ images were reconstructed using a soft-sensitivity encoding (SENSE) approach with the coil-sensitivity maps derived from the eigen decomposition of the calibration data. To preserve phase-coherence, only the main component of the decomposition was used for reconstruction. All images from the same acquisition were rigidly co-registered to the first TSL using Elastix (17). The ME model is defined as St=A⋅exp−tT1ρ,ME+η, where A is the complex-valued signal amplitude, T1ρ,ME denotes the average relaxation time, and η is the complex-valued Gaussian noise. The bi-exponential model (BE) assumes the existence of two pools of water in the same voxel, one pool with a fast relaxation time and the other with a slower relaxation time, and a parameter controlling the fraction of the total volume corresponding to the fast and slow components. The BE model is defined as St=A⋅f⋅exp−t T1ρ,s + 1−f ⋅exp−t T1ρ,l +η, where f denotes the fraction of the short relaxation time, T1ρ,s denotes the short relaxation time, and T1ρ,l the long relaxation time. In this model, the fast-relaxing pool is associated with macromolecule-bound water, and the slow-relaxing pool is associated with bulk water(11). A previous study suggested that, in early OA, both the signal intensity and relaxation times of each pool may change differently, and these changes could potentially improve sensitivity to disease changes(18).

An alternative to the bi-exponential model is the stretched-exponential model (SE)(19), that does not assume a specific number of pools but rather models the heterogeneity of the voxel with one additional parameter compared to the ME model. It is defined as St=A⋅exp−tT1ρ,SEα+η,α denotes the heterogeneity of voxel relaxation times. Its primary limitation is the difficulty in interpreting the heterogeneity parameter. Figure 1 illustrates these models along with examples of relaxation curves.

Using a non-linear least squares method implemented through in-house developed scripts in MATLAB (The MathWorks Inc., R2024a), the complex-valued images were fitted to three exponential models: ME, SE, and BE models. From previous work (11, 14), the model parameters were constrained to the following: T1ρ,ME and T1ρ,SE from 0.1ms to 400ms, α from 0.1 to 1.0, f from 0.01 to 0.99, BE T1ρ,s from 0.1 to 10ms, and BE T1ρ,l from 20 to 300ms.

To analyze the goodness of fit (GOF), the residual of the fitting (sum of squared errors) and the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) were computed. Since the SE and BE models include more parameters than the ME model, it is expected that residuals will be smaller and the maps act as a sanity check on the fitting process. The AIC was corrected by the number of parameters, and the corrected AIC (AICc) can be used to choose a model. The GOF analysis was not used to exclude voxels from the aggregation, and the reported values for each model consider all voxels, including the ones where the model was not the best fit as per AICc. If the longer relaxation time (or the single relaxation for ME and SE) reached the upper bound of the range, the voxel is eliminated from the aggregation.

The cartilage was segmented into three compartments, patellar cartilage, femoral cartilage, and tibial cartilage, using the Deep Open-Source Medical Image Analysis (DOSMA) framework segmentation tool(20), followed by manual inspection and refinement (HLDM, 4 years of experience). Based on the Quantitative Biomarkers Alliance (QIBA) profile(21), these compartments were further divided into six regions: patellar cartilage (PC), trochlear cartilage (TrC), lateral and medial femoral cartilage (LFC and MFC), and lateral and medial tibial cartilage (LTC and MTC). Figure 2 shows representative slices of a healthy subject and the segmentation mask used. The measurements were taken as the mean values of each cartilage segmented area and the mean values over the entire cartilage as a global area.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: The study was powered for the hypothesis of two-tailed group differences in globally averaged ME T1ρ with a Type I error rate of 5% and statistical power of 80%. Based on previous studies (8) and recommendations from the QIBA profile (21), we expect a mean T1ρ of about 40ms for the HS group, standard deviation of 3ms, and a minimum detectable effect of 7%. This resulted in a sample size of 19 subjects per group.

To isolate disease-related changes from confounders, the quantitative parameters were adjusted for age using a linear regression model (Y ~ Age + Group + Age × Group). Biological covariates, Sex and BMI, were also evaluated as potential confounders using nested F-tests but were only retained in the final model if they significantly improved the regression model fit. All preprocessing steps were performed within a nested cross-validation framework, ensuring that parameters were derived only from the training data to prevent data leakage.

For the univariate analysis, both groups were tested separately for data normality, and homoscedasticity using Shapiro-Wilk and Levene’s tests, respectively. As these assumptions were rejected, the Mann-Whitney U test was used to evaluate differences between groups for individual parameters. Effect sizes were calculated using Cliff’s delta. The p-values for the univariate analysis were corrected for the false discovery rate (FDR) using the Benjamini-Hochberg method across all evaluated regions and parameters.

The multivariate analysis was performed for all three models to assess the diagnostic value of combining spatial and, for the SE and BE models, multi-parametric information. Instead of performing a multivariate analysis of variance (MANOVA)(22), a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) was used to project the features into a single discriminant score(23). Both MANOVA and LDA methods are centered on the partitioning of the scatter matrix into the within-group and between-group matrices, from which the eigenvalue decomposition will lead to the eigenvalues, used in test statistics, and eigenvectors, that form the canonical discriminant functions(24). Given that the number of features can be as high as 18 for the multi-ROI bi-exponential model (3 model parameters for each of the 6 ROIs) and the expected correlation between cartilage regions, a regularized LDA (25) was employed to stabilize the covariance matrix estimation.

A nested cross-validation framework was implemented to provide an estimate of generalization performance. The framework, illustrated in Supplementary Figure 1, can be described by three stages: 1) Outer loop: a stratified 5-fold cross-validation used to separate training and testing sets; 2) Inner loop: using the training set of the outer loop, a 5-fold cross-validation was performed to optimize the regularized LDA and fit the linear model for covariate adjustment; 3) The best model and the preprocessing parameters from the inner loop were applied to the held-out test fold of the outer iteration.

LDA was first used to combine the models’ parameters into a single value for the global ROI. This was achieved by stacking each model’s parameters into an NxP matrix (N = number of datasets for training, P = number of parameters in the model). In the case of the ME model, LDA rescales the measurements to an arbitrary unit of measurement. Receiver Operating Characteristics (ROC) analysis(26) was performed using the data in the discriminator space, and performance was assessed by the Area Under the Curve (AUC). Bootstrapping with 5000 iterations was performed to evaluate the 95% confidence interval (CI) for the AUC scores of the final model obtained with the nested cross-validation framework. The ROC analysis also provided an optimal threshold for efficient classification, these optimal decision thresholds were calculated only for models demonstrating statistically significant discriminative performance (AUC significantly > 0.5, p < 0.05).

Finally, a similar analysis was evaluated while applying the LDA to an NxPR matrix (PR = P multiplied by the number of ROIs). This analysis reduced the dimensionality of PR to 1, using the information from the different cartilage compartments to increase group separability.
For the test statistics on the LDA-combined data, a non-parametric MANOVA using the eigenvalues from the combination was used. Effect sizes were calculated using Cliff’s delta with the U statistic.

Calibration curves and Decision Curve Analysis were also performed to assess the clinical utility of the multi-ROI models. Brier Scores (BS) were used to compare the calibration curves between models. Feature stability was evaluated based on the cross-validation folds and their importance verified using a permutation-based test with 1000 permutations.

The significance level was defined as p < 0.05, and the estimated p-values were corrected using the false discovery rate (FDR) correction. All tests were performed in MATLAB (The MathWorks Inc., R2024a) environment using built-in methods and the Fathom toolbox(27).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Registration quality showed good alignment between different TSLs as shown in Supplementary Figure 2, with the correlation coefficients between each TSL and the first dropped with increasing TSL as expected from the lower magnitude in the later images. The two repeated TSLs, 33 and 55 ms, showed good agreement between subsequent acquisitions.

Supplemental Figure 3 shows a representative slice with the GOF metrics obtained from a volunteer in the HS group. The fitting residuals decreased with the number of model parameters, with the BE model showing the smallest residuals voxel-wise. Still, when analyzing the AICc, the BE model showed the worst results. By choosing the model with the lowest AICc for each voxel, approximately 55% of the voxels were better represented by the SE model, while the ME model was chosen in approximately 45% of the voxels and the BE model in less than 1%.

Starting from the base linear regression model, the addition of sex as a main effect did not significantly improve the fit, with F=0.05, p=0.82, and ΔR2=0.001%. Adding BMI as a covariate resulted in an F=2.92, p=0.09, and ΔR2=0.051%. Given the limited sample size and the baseline variance explained by Age (ΔR2=22.2%, significant), model parsimony was prioritized, and the final adjustment model retained only age and the age-by-group interaction.

Figure 3 shows representative slices of the fitted parameters for an HS and an OA patient. Figure 4 illustrates the differences in measured parameters between groups across different ROIs. Table 2 summarizes the median and interquartile ranges for the three models’ parameters, along with p-values for the difference between the medians of the two groups and effect sizes. The parameter T1ρ,ME showed nearly significant differences between groups in the MTC (p=0.09) and LTC (p=0.07) ROIs. For the stretched-exponential model, the T1ρ,SE showed significant difference for the MTC ROI. The parameter α showed no significant differences in any of the ROIs: Global (p=0.12), PC (p=0.13), TrC (p=0.28), MFC (p=0.12), MTC (p=0.24), LFC (p=0.24), and LTC (p=0.28). The parameter T1ρ,l showed nearly significant difference (p=0.06) for the MTC ROI. The parameter f showed no significant differences between groups in any ROI: Global (p=0.69), PC (p=0.81), TrC (p=0.71), MFC (p=0.76), MTC (p=0.28), LFC (p=0.69), and LTC (p=0.71). The parameter T1ρ,s also showed no significant differences in any of the ROIS: Global (p=0.81), PC (p=0.81), TrC (p=0.89), MFC (p=0.28), MTC (p=0.87), LFC (p=0.87), and LTC (p=0.96).

Figure 5 illustrates the application of LDA to the SE model on the global ROI. The data points are also projected onto the axes to demonstrate group separation for the individual parameters. While considering the parameters separately, the effect sizes were 0.38 for T1ρ,SE and 0.36 for α, the LDA-combined SE parameter presented an effect size of 0.46.

Table 3 summarizes the results of the LDA-combined parameters using only the Global ROI and also multiple ROIs. For the Global ROI, no model showed significant differences between the HS and OA groups, with p-values = 0.15, 0.84, and 0.47 for the ME, SE, and BE models, respectively. When combining all ROIs, the SE model showed a significant difference between groups and an effect size of 0.43. The ME model showed a nearly significant difference with a p=0.06 and effect size of 0.35. The BE model showed non-significant difference with a p=0.84.

Figure 6 illustrates the early OA detection performance of the LDA-combined biomarkers assessed with ROC analysis. The LDA-combined parameters on the global ROI resulted in mean AUCs and CIs of 0.57 [0.28, 0.59], 0.50 [0.35, 0.66], and 0.56 [0.30, 0.59] for the ME, SE, and BE models, respectively. None of the models presented performance significantly different from random assignment, with FDR adjusted p-values of 0.67, 0.96, and 0.67 for the ME, SE, and BE models.

When LDA was used to combine the model parameters over all sub-regions, the mean AUCs and CIs obtained were 0.63 [0.47, 0.78] (p=0.14), 0.83 [0.72, 0.93] (p<0.05), and 0.54 [0.30, 0.61] (p=0.60), for the ME, SE, and BE models, respectively. By choosing a threshold point that lies closest to the top left corner of the graph, or (0, 1), the ME model resulted in a sensitivity of 0.517 and a specificity of 0.759. The SE model resulted in a sensitivity of 0.690 and specificity of 0.759. The stability of the weights for each feature is shown in Supplementary Figure 4. The permutation-based test resulted in significant weight importance only in the SE model.

Figure 7 shows the performance of the multi-ROI models using calibration curves and decision curve analysis. The Brier Scores were 0.26, 0.17, and 0.34 for the ME, SE, and BE models, respectively. The difference between ME and SE scores was significant. The decision curve shows the net benefit of each model compared to predicting every subject as OA (Treat All) or as HS (Treat None) strategies. The ME model showed marginal benefit in the threshold probability range of 0.42 and 0.64, while the SE model improved on the standard strategies and the ME model in the range of 0.12 and 0.87.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The primary finding of the study was that the detection of early OA was significantly improved by using a multi-regional analysis of the parameters derived from the SE model. While the ME model is widely used, the results presented in this study indicate that in a multi-regional analysis, the SE model significantly outperformed both the ME and the BE models. Even though the BE model provided the lowest fitting residuals, it failed to discriminate between groups. This could be due to its higher feature-to-sample ratio. The SE model also presented superior calibration and net benefit in the Decision Curve analysis. The novelty of this study lies in demonstrating that the diagnostic performance gain of non-mono-exponential models is only achieved when combined with spatial specificity.

Previous studies(28, 29) have used non-mono-exponential models for T1ρ and T2∗ relaxation time mapping, demonstrating significant differences between case and control groups(28) as well as significant correlations with patient-reported outcome measures (PROM)(29). These are important results that highlight the applicability of bi-exponential models, but these studies did not delve into whether the bi-exponential model can improve the sensitivity of detection, separability of groups, or correlations with PROMs.

The results presented here confirm that globally averaging T1ρ obscures the local signal changes associated with early degeneration, as no globally averaging model achieved significance. On the contrary, the LDA assigned higher weights to the LTC, PC, and MTC compartments, which aligns with the known compartmental distribution of knee OA(30). This supports the view that cartilage assessment must be spatially specific to be a viable biomarker (6). By treating different compartments as distinct variables, we can effectively capture patterns of disease that could be stronger predictors than the magnitude of a global average alone. Similar conclusions were found in studies that analyzed local T2 texture (31), regional patterns in T1ρ (32), and cartilage thickness(33).

Although sex as a covariate did not make it to the final model, it cannot be completely discarded. A study by Kumar et al. (34) showed significant differences between men and women with knee OA. This discrepancy could be due to sample size, they recruited 43 OA patients, but it could also be due to a difference in KL grades for the inclusion criteria. In their study, the OA group included 20 people graded KL3, and 4 graded KL. The inclusion criteria for the OA group in the present study were KL grades 1 and 2. This difference could be the cause of the significant sex-related differences in the reports.

Another contribution of the present study is validating the SE model’s heterogeneity parameter (α) as a diagnostic marker. Unlike T1ρ, which reflects the average interaction between water and macromolecules, α quantifies the deviation from a single exponential decay, effectively acting as a proxy for microstructural complexity(19). In early OA, the disruption of the collagen network and proteoglycan loss likely increases this microstructural heterogeneity, by mixing bulk water pools with water pools that are bound by the fragmented network. The results here suggest that this disorder can be captured by the SE model and can provide a diagnostic advantage over the ME model that assumes a homogeneous microenvironment. The superior performance of the SE model over the BE model can be a direct result of two factors: 1) fewer parameters to fit using a reduced number of points, and 2) a more numerically stable model. Combined, these two factors can influence the variability of the fitted parameters, resulting in less perturbation in the SE parameters when compared to BE. Additionally, it can be thought that the SE model also results in a better representation of the degraded cartilage tissue with its heterogeneity parameter when compared to ME.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study included a small sample size (N=52) which greatly limited the statistical power and generalization of the results. This sample size also prevents a deeper analysis of the effects of sex and BMI in the models. Still, this work served as proof-of-concept for the combination of multi-parametric and sub-region analysis for the early detection of OA, and the use of a nested cross-validation framework mitigated the risk of overfitting in the results.

There was a significant sex imbalance in the OA group. While sex was evaluated as a covariate and found to be non-significant, the sample size limited the statistical power to detect subtle sex-specific differences.

The labeling of the study population relied on radiographic KL grading and clinical symptoms. Lacking arthroscopic validation means that it is not possible to strictly rule out pre-radiographic changes in the healthy group. Furthermore, cartilage segmentation refinement was performed by a single investigator and, therefore, inter-rater reliability was not assessed.

This study was performed in a single center using a single scanner and vendor. As quantitative MRI values can be vendor and field-strength dependent, the absence of external validation limits the generalization of these results to other centers until further validation. While repeatability and test-retest analysis were not performed in the present study, the sequence and fitting models used here were previously validated(11, 14).

The use of LDA in this study likely violated a few assumptions, such as normality, no correlation between variables, among others, still, it is a relatively robust tool. Finally, morphological analysis, such as cartilage volume or thickness, was not performed as the focus of the work was on compositional changes at early stages of disease.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study demonstrated that by applying a sub-regional analysis to Stretched-Exponential T1ρ mapping significantly improved the performance for distinguishing healthy knees from those diagnosed with early osteoarthritis when compared to the more standard mono-exponential. In contrast, the bi-exponential model proved unstable and ineffective, likely due to overfitting caused by the small sample size used. These findings support the adoption of multi-regional models as potential biomarkers for early OA, pending validation in larger cohorts.

ack.title: Grant Support:
ack.p: This study was financially supported by NIH grants R01-AR076328, R01-AR076985, and R01-AR078308, and was performed under the rubric of the Center of Advanced Imaging Innovation and Research (CAI2R) at NYU Grossman School of Medicine, a NIBIB Biomedical Technology Resource Center (NIH P41 EB017183).

fn.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest to disclose with regard to this study.

ZibettiMVW, MenonRG, de MouraHL, ZhangX, KijowskiR, RegatteRR: Updates on Compositional MRI Mapping of the Cartilage: Emerging Techniques and Applications. Journal of Magnetic Resonance Imaging 2023; 58.10.1002/jmri.28689PMC1032370037010113

caption.p: Illustration of the three exponential models, mono-exponential, stretched-exponential, and bi-exponential (ME, SE, and BE), used to fit the T1ρ data. Both the SE and BI models reduce the fitting error compared to the ME model, although that does not mean they have a better clinical application.

Example of segmentation performed in a healthy volunteer, displaying the 6 regions of interest (ROIs): medial femoral cartilage (MFC), medial tibial cartilage (MTC), patellar cartilage (PC), throclear cartilage (TrC), lateral femoral cartilage (LFC), and lateral tibial cartilage (LTC).

Representative parameter maps for each of the three models (ME, SE, and BE) from one healthy subject and an early osteoarthritis patient. Cooler (blue) colors indicate lower relaxation times associated with healthy tissue, while warmer (red) colors indicate higher relaxation times associated with degeneration.

Boxplot charts for each model’s parameters for global and ROI measurements. The * indicates significant differences between the healthy (HS) and osteoarthritis (OA) groups, represented by blue and orange boxes, respectively.

Illustration of the use of linear discriminant analysis (LDA) to combine the global average of SE model parameters. The dashed box separates the scatterplot from the two projections of data points onto the axes. When considering a single parameter, the separability of the two groups underperformed the separability when considering the two dimensions. By projecting the data points onto the line orthogonal to the LDA boundary, maximum separability of the two groups was achieved.

The combination of parameters using LDA for the Global ROI and for combining all ROIs. The stretched-exponential presented marginally better area under the curve (AUC) values than the ME model at the Global ROI but presented good improvement over the ME model with the Multi-ROI analysis. The bi-exponential model showed poor performance for the Global ROI but showed improvement at the Multi-ROI analysis.

Assessment of clinical utility of Multi-ROI T1ρ models. The calibration curves (A) show the SE model as the best with a Brier Score of 0.22, followed by the ME model with a Brier Score of 0.24. The BE model showed a poor calibration curve, with a Brier Score of 0.34, demonstrating overfitting. (B) Decision Curve Analysis also demonstrates the net benefit of the models compared to the treat-all and treat-none strategies. The ME model showed benefit gains in the threshold probability range of [0.37, 0.6], while the SE model showed gains in the range of [0.3, 0.7]. The BE model performed worse than a treat-all strategy.
Demographics of the recruited subjects. Significant differences in the sex and age distributions were found, but no difference in body mass index (BMI).

Median parameter values and interquartile ranges for all three models, mono-, stretched-, and bi-exponential (ME, SE, and BE), for both healthy subjects (HS) and early osteoarthritis (OA) groups, along with the p-values from the Mann-Whitney U-test between groups and effect size (es). Bold values indicate significant differences. The represented regions of interest are: medial femoral cartilage (MFC), medial tibial cartilage (MTC), patellar cartilage (PC), trochlear cartilage (TrC), lateral femoral cartilage (LFC), and lateral tibial cartilage (LTC).

fn.p: ME – Mono-exponential, SE – Stretched-exponential, BE – Bi-exponential, LDA – Linear Discriminant Analysis, ROI – Region Of Interest, es – effect size, HS – Healthy Subject, OA – Osteoarthritis patient, MFC – Medial Femoral Cartilage, MTC – Medial Tibial Cartilage, PC – Patellar Cartilage, LFC – Lateral Femoral Cartilage, LTC – Lateral Tibial Cartilage, TrC – Throclea Cartilage.

caption.p: Median values, Inter-Quartile Ranges, p-values, and effect sizes (es) for the different LDA-combined parameters either at the Global ROI or at the combined Multi ROI. False Discovery Rate (FDR) was used to adjust the p-values. Significant results are presented in boldface.

fn.p: HS – Healthy Subject, OA – Osteoarthritis patient, ME – Mono-exponential, SE – Stretched-exponential, BE – Bi-exponential, LDA – Linear Discriminant Analysis, ROI – Region Of Interest, es – effect size.
